sentences.text: My name is Milton, M-I-L-T-O-N.
Schwartz, S-C-H-W-A-R-T-Z.
My name at birth was Mendel, M-E-N-D-E-L, Swarc, S-W-A-R-C.
My birth date is the 24th of June, 1926.
Now I am 69 years old.
The city where I was born is-- used to be Poland.
And it's spelled L-U-C-K, Luck.
And the country is Poland.
The state is Volhynia.
Life in Luck, prior to 1939, we were citizens of Poland.
We had-- the Jewish population of Luck was the majority.
I would assume there were more than 55% or 58% of the population.
And life in, in, in Poland were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] really pleasant for the Jewish people.
We had our own synagogues.
We had-- we could go to the synagogue whenever you wanted to.
We had our own schools.
The majority of Jewish children went to yeshiva, where they studied Hebrew, Torah.
And they studied-- and they had to study Polish.
It was required to study Polish, too.
But some of the children went to public schools.
The yeshivas by us were free of charge.
Whatever child could not afford-- or the parents could not afford to pay-- they let them-- they, they begged the children to come to school.
And the city would support them.
The 'city' means the Jewish agencies of the city of Luck made sure that every child could get a Jewish education.
If you wanted to, you could get a Jewish education.
Like I said before, only one requirement was that they had to study the Polish language, too.
And the friends of ours-- the majority of the children we played with were Jewish.
Naturally, we played with our neighbors-- were Poles or Ukrainians.
The majority of the city, as I said before, was Jewish people.
We even had beaches to go on the beach to go swimming summertime.
The Jewish kids went swimming in a different section of the-- of the beach, and the Gentile kids went to swim in their own section of the beach.
We were not segregated, but we kept to our-- to ourselves.
And the life was very, very pleasant.
Poor-- we were very poor.
But when we were young, we did not know what-- how poor it was.
But we were very happy.
My family consists of father and mother.
And we had-- they have six children.
I was the third child.
The first was Ruchel.
She was born in, in 1920.
My, my older brother was Liezer, he was born in 1923 or '24, I don't-- I don't remember it now so well.
Maybe '24, because he was only-- nearly two years older than me.
And I was third child.
Then was another girl, Maya.
She was-- when the time when they were-- killed her, she was probably 12 years old.
There was a little brother, Abraham.
He was 10.
And there, a little, small brother-- blond little boy, David.
He was five years old.
My father worked in a brewery as electrical engineer, electrician.
Engineering-- he worked in the engineering department as electrician.
And he worked in the brewery many, many years.
He worked since the First World War, yet.
There were two breweries.
One was brewery by a Czech.
The name was a-- was a, a Czechish brewery.
And the brewery my father worked was a Jewish brewery by the [?
Schneider ?] brothers.
They were the owners of the brewery.
They-- we lived in [NON-ENGLISH], in the part of the city which was adjacent, not far from the brewery.
All my brothers and sisters attended religious schools, the yeshivas.
My sister attended a, a, a girls' school, and the boys attended boys' school because the, the religious schools were segregated, boys separate and girls separate.
My mother--
--was a housewife.
She stayed home and made sure that we have something to eat, and there was some food at home, of course.
Well, since we were the majority of kids in the city, in my hometown, there was not too much problem with the kids.
There was incidents where I was beaten up by, by a Gentile kid, just simply because I was Jewish.
But we kept together.
We went home from school together.
As a matter of fact, when I went to school, I was a strong kid.
So all the children wait for me to come home with [LAUGHS] them in order that I should be able to-- because by us, the fights were only fistfights.
There were no knives or guns.
This didn't exist, only fistfights.
You got a beating, got a black eye, but this is all.
But if you are a good athlete and a good-- like I was-- a good ballplayer, soccer player-- I was accepted.
Whatever they played in a group, and they needed extra kids, they even took the Jewish kids in to play.
So they were very much in favor for athletic kids.
And we were-- we got along.
I spoke Polish fluently, as good as the Poles did.
And we got along.
But there was prejudices, quietly.
But we, we took it.
This is the way it's supposed to be.
We are foreigners.
And we took it the way life has continued this way.
I understand that this-- when I used to go with my father to listen to in the synagogue, to listen to what was going on, what's going on in Germany.
As I said to some people, some friends I know here in America, that we did not imagine that bad like that could happen.
We know there are discriminations.
There are demonstrations.
They don't let any people go into the stores.
But this was only till 1939.
We knew, for example, about the, the Kristallnacht.
We knew-- we demonstrated on the street.
They are getting the Germans [INAUDIBLE] again the Jews.
But we figured that the Jewish people in Germany were only a quarter percent.
The majority of them had opportunity to leave.
So this is the way by us-- we were 10% of the Polish population.
In Poland, they was more magnanimous to the Jews because we were a big part of the population.
And so we were-- we did not feel the, the, the, the tragedy that was going on in Germany.
The reason was, because when the tragedies actually happened later on, the Russians occupied our hometown in 1939.
Then all the news from Germany-- derogatory against Germany-- stopped.
Nothing.
Cut off from Germany.
Now, for example, the, the, the, the, the Russians were gloating with happiness that the Germans are destroying so many ships in England.

And the Germans are doing so many [? tonnage ?] to the Germans, and the Germans are doing so-- because, if you recall, when the Germans-- when the Russians came into my hometown, they had a pact, a secret pact between the Russians and the Germans.
And the River Bug, which divided part of the area where the Russians occupied, became Vistula, Ukraine.
They renamed it.
It used to be Poland, and then they named it Vistula, Ukraine.
And they stopped at the river.
They did not identify between the German Armies and the Russians because the agreement was done secretly.
We didn't even know about it.
And then all privileges that the Jewish people had in Poland stopped.
Not that the Russians were-- discriminated against because they happened to be Jewish, but they arrived and enacted all kinds of anti-religious laws.
And we suffered very much about that because the majority of people in my hometown were religious.
And the Russians came out as a scheme to make a five-day week work.
And five-day means that every second week is another, another day off.
For example, one day Sunday, one day Monday, one day Tuesday.
So in order to do away with the Sunday and Saturday-- I don't know how the Christian population was-- took it, but we Jewish people took it very much to heart.
And the trouble was, my father received a letter from America-- my aunt, my mother's sister.
They kept my father for three nights and days in the NKVD.
And they questioned him why he receives letters.
They were very paranoid about espionage.
My father had to make a proclamation-- made a signed paper that he is going to send a letter to America.
They should never write to us anymore.
And we cut any connection with America because we were afraid for our lives.
No, no, no, no.
The, the, the Poles were very much pressed-- very much with the Germans.
Any Pole who run away from the German area, or any Polish citizen who run away, were happy that at least the Russians came in.

But I understand that a day, two days later, when the Russians came in, they disarmed all the Polish soldiers, and they took them-- so especially, they took a lot of-- in the PWs, and especially captains and officers.

Which, it happened after that, that the famous and the tragic act in, in, in Katyn, Katyn, Russia, that-- and unfortunately, because all the people were mobilized in Poland to fight against the, the, the Germans, the majority of the officers in the Polish Army happened to be Jewish professionals-- doctors, lawyers, dentists.
So the result was that in Katyn, about 88% of the officers that the Russians killed were Jewish people.
Because there were-- they were-- they were inducted into the army during the war, taken from their offices.
And they were in the intelligentsia.
They were the professionals.
The, the, the-- this was a tragic-- a tragic case that happened.
How did the Russian occupation affect your schooling?
The Russian occupation affected our schooling in a way that we had to stop short any, any religious education.
So I signed up to high school.
In Russian, it's called [RUSSIAN].
I was very young then.
[RUSSIAN] means 10-year school-- high school and public school together.
And I had to go and study general studies.
It means everything in Russian, nothing Jewish.
The Jewish schools right way closed.
The yeshivas, they closed.
They curtailed the, the, the Jewish people going to the synagogues.
So the Jewish people had to-- had to pray Saturdays in their own-- in their own houses, with people getting together and praying.
There was not such to do-- they did only-- not only to the Jewish people, they could tell the people going to the churches, too.
They were trying to indoctrinate the people against their religion.
But I, as a child, I knew that I went to school.
I was a very good student at school.
The school, there was no discrimination.
All the school-- the children went together in school with the Gentile, Jewish kids together.
Before, I was only strict in a Jewish school.
And we studied.
And we learned.
We didn't have a too-long time to catch up to see what happened.
But the, the war started in 1941.
My father was able to continue work in the brewery.
And he got paid for the year that he worked in the brewery without, without getting a vacation.

So the Russian government had forced the owners of the brewery-- whatever the money they had, they should pay out a, a month of cash because of the years that passed that my father worked in the brewery for 25 years, he didn't get paid.
So he should at least get paid for the holiday-- for the vacation days.
So we, we, we got a little money.
My-- we bought a cow.
Then we bought another cow.
The life was a little bit better by the Russians than by the Poles because of the compensation that my father has gotten.
But the-- and we felt much more happier as, as Jewish kids on the street.
The discrimination against kids were subdued because they were-- they would not allow anybody to beat somebody up because he happened to be another, another, another nationality.
Because this was-- the fear of the people were very much stronger during the Russians than the fear of the Poles.
The Russians would not allow this, this, this kind of excesses should be committed.
For a better life, yeah.
Financially--
Financially improved, because as I said, under one condition, because my father get a big stipend.
I don't know how much.
But all of a sudden, we lived better.
We had better to eat because a cow means a lot of milk.
And we had cheeses.
We had all kind of milk products, that my mother had more food for us to, to-- this way, we used to eat some better food than we used to for Poland.
Poland, we ate potatoes most of the time.
But by the Russians, the life became a little bit better.
There was-- this is the tragic that I start thinking all the time, what has happened?
The night of the night when the Germans attacked the Russians, the Russian Army-- this what I know for a fact-- it was sold.
The whole front-- everything was sold, sold out.
The Russian general gave a order to the tank brigade, which was in my hometown.
They should empty the-- change the oil in the tank, empty the tanks, and refill with oil the next day.
The same night, the German attacked at 4 o'clock in the morning.
We got up at night, and the houses were shaking.
As a matter of fact, two bombs flew across-- fell down across our house.
One fell to the front of the house about 200 yards.
And one fell in-- [LAUGHS] fell in the-- in the outhouse, where you go because--
--there's no toilet.
Yeah, outhouse [LAUGHS] in the-- and, and we were very much in fear.
And all of a sudden, the war started.
And we were so gullible.
So, so we, we didn't-- we didn't believe.
My father-- nobody believed that a might like the Russians, with big tanks and big cannons-- and the Germans are occupied with the English and the French.
They're already fighting a war-- would destroy the Russian Army.
We wouldn't have believe it.
And the propaganda from Moscow was so strong because every citizen in the-- in my hometown had a radio, like a speaker.
Only what [INAUDIBLE] the busker wanted to listen you could hear.
But you cannot have your own radio.
You're not supposed to have your own radio.
So the propaganda was so big that our army went into Warsaw.
Our army took the town of Warsaw.
But we were so gullible, so stupid.
We could see the Russians running half-naked on the street.
We could see the Russian citizens that came to work in my hometown out packing their valises, and gathering up in their trucks, and running away to Russia.
We could see it.
My father-- the golden opportunity to grab a horse and a wagon in the brewery where he worked.
Put the kids in the top.
Take the car on the back of the-- of the-- of the-- of the-- of the-- of, of the carriage, and ran.
Ran as far as we can get away from the front.
And four days later, Wednesday afternoon, I passed by the street to go to get some food for the cows.
I saw the Germans walking with their machine guns over their chests, and with their sleeves.
[INAUDIBLE] And they're shooting everybody, whatever, on sight.
They probably didn't shoot me because I was a little kid.
So they didn't because I saw them coming in.
And they did-- came in from the other side of the city, not from where they're supposed to come from the west.
They came in from the east.
They surrounded the city, surrounded all the old Russian Armies.
And they took millions and thousands upon thousands of Russian soldiers and, and PWs.
And this where I get very upset, when I read a article of a, a German writer-- Aran, Arendt, or something like this.
I forgot her name.
And she wrote, why didn't the Jewish people stand up and fight against the Germans?
Here is our army of people who had a gun just in their hands.
And I saw three Germans.
One German horse in the front, one German to the side, another German on the side on a horse here, leading millions of people and PWs, soldiers.
And not only that, the Germans-- which I'll tell you later after I explain to you about the ghettos-- where the Germans imposed upon us the collective-- the collective punishment.
It's not like I saw a demonstration, demonstration in, in, in Warsaw-- in Washington, DC.
In the name of the law, please disperse, begging the people to disperse their demonstrating.
The Germans wouldn't say 'the name of the law.' The Germans opened up machine guns and, and kill 10,000, 5,000 people on the spot.
And then, this a collective punishment.
In June, the Germans came in.
As soon as they came in, the Wehrmacht came in, the soldiers.
And then we find that first day, they killed this man, or they killed this man.
A man went out the back of the house.
They killed him--
Originally, they apparently cared.
They cared from beginning because they had a pact with the Ukrainians, too-- the Germans already.
They had Ukrainians in Germany that they are going to make a [NON-ENGLISH], a independent Ukraine.
So now, the Germans promised them.
They never kept the promise, but they promised them.
So our biggest enemies and our biggest-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it-- [? destructions ?] of our people by the population living in-- around-- in our area.
Not as much as the Poles, because the Poles were there, strangers themselves.
Even so, it was Poland because this was Ukrainian land.
This was [NON-ENGLISH] Ukraine.
After the First World War, they allotted it to Poland, but the Ukrainians were the majority.
They were the majority.
So they, they, they, they-- at first, they started right away when the Germans came in, but not as systematically kill-- start killing.
This family was-- this father was killed.
This son was killed.
This woman went--
For example, they gave out all kind of orders.
A Jew is supposed to have a, a, a armband with a Star of David on his arm.
This wasn't enough.
Then they put a, a disk, a yellow disk, about 4 inches wide, yellow like this yellow, and, and a disk on the back, on the shoulder.
Why the disk and this together?
I don't know, because other places, they had only a disk on them.
And they were so brutalizing the population in order to put a fear in the-- in the people's minds they should never thought about resisting.
Because for any resistance, any little resistance, they would-- they would kill you for, for, for just like this.
And naturally, if anybody were to arm-- for example, a, a, a Jewish kid, he was a friend of my who was about a year younger than me.
But he was a very strong kid.
He was working and boxing and stuff like this.
I forgot his name.
God should forgive me.
And he was beaten by a German SS man because he didn't take off his hat for the German-- [GERMAN], the hat.
So he beat him.
He beat him.
That kid got so angry that he jumped at them, probably.
And he started biting him by the throat.
I don't know if he killed him or not.
He fell.
Then another Germans came along.
They killed him on the spot.
And then came Kommando, SS Kommando Jeeps, and they took out people from the houses at, at random.
And they killed them on the spot.
This is the punishment what-- if you lift a finger against a German.
And the women and the men and the kids were kicking him.
He was lying there dead, too, on the floor.
You, because of you, my father is killed.
Because of you-- if you wouldn't have hit the German, oh, did [INAUDIBLE] the Germans, they would have survived.
But the end result was anyway there were no survivor.
But there was no way.
They, they, they destroyed the village if we would fight.
They starved them to death.
They pushed them in ghettos.
The ghetto start, and they do in December time.
In the summertime, what is happening is the, the Germans and Ukrainians decided they want to kill off some people from the street, from the-- there are too many Jews in the town.
So they start catching Jews on the street.
For that, if a Jew walks, if he were recognizable, they catch him.
I was-- I happened to be unfortunate.
I went to get some food in the brewery-- the mold from the brewery-- the cows.
My mother sent me.
So they caught me.
And they take me-- take me-- they walk with me-- walk me down to the river.
And they sat me down to the river.
There were hundreds of people already.
I would say maybe, maybe thousands, maybe more than--
In July.
This was the bloody Thursday.
I remember the, the, the, the, the, the, the day of-- the day of the week, but I've forgot the day of the-- of the-- of the year because I didn't look up the book.
I have a book of my hometown.
So they brought a lot of people-- a lot of people there.
All of a sudden, I see trucks are coming.
Trucks-- and in it, they probably want to take us.
Like, I-- and, and, and I'm sitting-- right away, I was sitting there.
I didn't have any shoes.
I was barefooted, short pants, a little blouse.
It was warm, easy to swim.
And I'm near the river.
And the river has a very big peak of grass.
I picked up a big-- a big piece of grass, and I made a, a tube out of it.
And I start blowing while the other people are crying.
Oh, my wife doesn't know what happened.
The kids are starving.
I went to look for food.
And they're crying.
All men, all men, not a single girl.
Only men, only old men-- old, young, but not children, men.
But I was already a 15-year-old boy, a strong boy.
They picked me up, too.
And, and all of a sudden, a German comes over to other German, and says to him, hast du Feuer?
He says, ja, ja, ja wohl.
And he comes over to me, bends down by me, lit a light-- a lighter he had.
So I had the opportunity to sneak in the water.
And I slipped in the water, bit a straw.
I swam under the water.
And the water turned like this.
The river was very fierce-- the River Styr, it goes through our hometown.
And I escaped.
After a day or two, I somehow-- I was afraid to walk in daytime.
They may pick me up again.
So I sneak through the night.
I came home.
My parents were astonished.
What happened?
Where were you?
And I told them what had happened.
So all of a sudden, people find out that I was one of the group that the Germans picked up on the riverbank.
So they stood in line-- and, and waiting for me to tell them what happened.
And I couldn't tell what happened to them.
I escaped.
I said what happened.
And I was one of the 3,000 victims.
Which I said [? what I-- ?] hurt my heart to see when I was there in 1989.
And I went to the same place, to, to see the [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means-- the king of Poland, Lubartow, built a big castle in 1900 because it belonged to Poland, the town of Luck.
So in this, this castle, they figured they're going to, to, to kill off the Jews.
And I saw, to my astonishment, a man dressed up in, in a-- in a Ukrainian uniform, with a big mustache, the real [NON-ENGLISH], and chains on the hands, and chains on his feet.
And on the top was written down in Ukraine and Russian, which I know well, well, how to read because I studied Ukrainian and Russian.
I was going to school.
So it says, here the Germans tortured and killed 3,000 Ukrainians.
There was not a single Ukrainian killed there.
They were all Jews.
Even my, my-- and my friend of mine was there.
His father was killed there.
He went to see a cottage on the spot where the graves are inside of this castle area.
And he said, look what they're doing.
They're taking the Holocaust of the Jewish people.
They're making it a Holocaust of Ukrainians where the murderers became the victims.
--which is-- unfortunately, this was happening.
No.
When I came back, no.
After the war we find out what had happened because there are-- there were people who-- from the Ukrainian, from the Poles, who witnessed it, came and told.
Of course, historical come up, and it comes along, one and one.
And then the, the Russians kept on digging out the graves and find out other people are buried there.
So this came out.
But before that, we did not know.
Now, in, in, in--
We were in July--
We had this--
Yes.
The rest of my family is this-- is this that I want to explain to you.
It was a-- tragic what the Germans have done.
First of all, they make us-- they made all kinds of different laws, the worst laws that degrade you to make you like a-- like a subhuman.
Because the Germans considered us Jewish people, with the Russians included, unfortunately, Untermenschen.
Explanation English-- sub-humans.
Easier to do the killing, to kill us.
And they in-- all, all kinds of orders.

For example, if your family had six kids, they made a law about this-and-this date later on, if anybody is missing from this, this house at night when they come to check, they killed the whole family to instill a fear in you that nobody should run away from the house, go into the woods, become a partisan, or start fighting against the Germans.
They couldn't do it to the Gentile population-- that they do it to us.
They made everybody responsible for each other.
So any good son-- like, for example, myself.
My father sneaked me out before the ghetto was formed.
Later on, he sneaked me away to a village.
He couldn't have done it later after this law would have enacted because then I would be afraid to leave in order not to harm my father and mother, my brothers and sisters.
Because then it would be-- it would be out of the question.
They made so much-- so many laws against every-- water to be gotten.
You have to get it in, in, in-- and you have to stay in line to get water.
